title: Writing about Evangelion to an unfamiliar audience
display_title: Writing about Evangelion to an unfamiliar audience
wikitext:
[]: Writing about Evangelion to an unfamiliar audience             Quote Post by chaosakita » Wed Dec 29, 2010 12:15 pm I am trying to write a critical analysis about EoE to an audience that probably won't know about the source material. I guess I've done my best to try to distill the relevant parts of the plot within the film, but I'm not sure what parts of the series I should include to help the readers understand. For instance, I have written about the masturbation scene, but should I include that part of the reason that Shinji is so distraught is that he was forced to kill his only remaining friend? What about a brief summary of Shinji and Asuka's relationship? What do you guys think is important? Here is some of what I have so far: To begin with, the film begins with an infamous scene of the distraught protagonist, Shinji, masturbating to the body of his comatose friend Asuka, which sets the tone for what is to follow. The rest of the narrative is marked by brief moments of triumph only to be ultimately subverted, as well as a prevailing sense of helplessness. At the beginning of the film, the heroes have just recently defeated the ironically-named, Lovecraftian “angels”, only to be then be attacked by the brutally sadistic human soldiers of Seele, who brutally slaughter hundreds of innocents to achieve their goals. The animation leaves very little to the imagination. Despite their best efforts, the heroes know that defeat is inevitable. Asuka miraculously manages to break out of her coma and seems to gain the upper hand in defeating her aggressors, but she is not just defeated, but rather, eaten alive by the biological weapons Seele has sent. The rest of the characters can only watch. Eventually, the entire population of the world is virtually killed as a victorious Seele commences their plan of “Instrumentality”, the forced amalgamation of all souls into a single consciousness. The remaining characters can only watch in horror as their souls are absorbed. Even when Shinji is able to break out of Instrumenality and return to the real world, the viewers are only left with an ambiguous final scene of Shinji and a mysteriously returned Asuka lying on a desolate beach together, seemingly the only two people in existence, . Upon realizing the presence of Asuka, Shinji begins to choke her, but stops. Asuka then says, “I feel sick,” and without any further explanation, the film ends.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Dec 29, 2010 1:24 pm I've written reviews for Evangelion, a lot of them look like this . Anytime I go in-depth about a movie in any critical way, I just go all out and put a spoiler warning in front of the article. Read a few of David Bordwell's in-depth film reviews if you really want some references for critical analysis writing about film. He'll even go through a film shot-by-shot, scene-by-scene, and explore every technique in order to find the film's message and how the filmmaker wants his audience to feel about the message presented in the film. As for the question of adding in elements to the film, like, say Shinji's emotional response to killing Kaworu, go for it. When writing something that goes in-depth into the analysis of the film, you need everything written out to explore properly the relation of all the elements that lead to the film's final conclusion. Just put a spoiler warning up in front of the article to make sure no one feels cheated out of ... whatever they feel cheated out of when they hear or read spoilers.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Wed Dec 29, 2010 1:27 pm Thanks! I'm writing about the movie for a paper, so there's a limit of how much I can write. I have to both be able to explain the series and form my argument in the word limit, so I'm not sure what exactly are the most important things to add.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Dec 29, 2010 2:34 pm Then you'd probably want to go for an in-depth review. I'm not sure what you're word count is, but my word count for the combined review and the comparative essay for Evangelion 1.11 ended up being over 2,400 words. So you'd probably want to hit the highlights of the film and explore how the film shows over-all depth of character and concept. You probably wouldn't want to explore very single character turning point or shot or anything like that, but just the broad over-arcing characters path of Shinji while focusing on the key points that get them to the conclusion. Treat the rest of the cast as supporting your argument as to why Shinji does what he does, but don't focus on any one character other than Shinji. (A.K.A., avoid focusing too much on Asuka, even though the series itself spends all of episode 22 on her.) You might want to touch briefly on the religious cameos throughout, and the style of cinematography and how it's slightly different from most other shows. But again, only do that when it supports an argument or point about the over-all plot. Don't focus on any of them too much. It's also really helpful when you remember that the entire plot of NGE can be summarized into one sentence: A timid 14-year-old boy is forced to fight against the Angels. Same with The End of Evangelion , as technically humans were supposed to be the 18th Angel (whether it be metaphorically or biologically), and have yet remained undefeated until EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by Jornophelanthas » Wed Dec 29, 2010 2:46 pm How many words are you allowed to use? Use half of them for exposition of the movie, and the other half for analysis. Most likely, you won't be able to fully address every theme and sub-theme (or even have room to mention all of them). Instead, focus on a single theme you wish to cover comprehensively. Such as the concept of Human Instrumentality and Shinji's acceptance/rejection of it. Or Shinji's trouble connecting to people (use Schopenhauer's hedgehog dilemma). Or Shinji's issues with his mother and father figures (use Freud's Oedipus complex for that one). Or Asuka's mother-trauma and her final blaze. Or Ritsuko's relationship with Gendo and her mother (leaving out the snake pit that is Rei for these relationships). Or the meaning of Rei and her four incarnations (Rei I, Rei II, Rei III, Lilith). Really. Less is more. Also, the character development all takes place in the anime series, not in End of Evangelion itself. The movie is basically a stunning barrage of climaxes to each character's development. Therefore, refer to relevant parts of the anime series.

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Wed Dec 29, 2010 3:09 pm I have only 1500 words for my essay. I'm supposed to write one argument about it, which mine is going to be about rejection of suicide + worth of living. Primarily, I'm going to write about Shinji and Asuka (especially because of their final scene together). Jornophelanthas wrote: Also, the character development all takes place in the anime series, not in End of Evangelion itself. The movie is basically a stunning barrage of climaxes to each character's development. Therefore, refer to relevant parts of the anime series.

[]:             Quote Post by Jornophelanthas » Wed Dec 29, 2010 4:32 pm chaosakita wrote: I have only 1500 words for my essay. I'm supposed to write one argument about it, which mine is going to be about rejection of suicide + worth of living. Primarily, I'm going to write about Shinji and Asuka (especially because of their final scene together).

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Dec 31, 2010 6:37 am I think it would be nearly impossible to condense anything substantial in NGE down to 1500 words when writing to an audience who's unfamiliar with its story, characters, overall setting and story, etc. It's just much too vast. But then trying to go into a detailed thematic analysis? Geez, I can empathize. I tried to write a summary of many of my thoughts about NGE here , even though I didn't touch on the themes and it's much longer than 1500 words. I would agree with Jornophelanthas' outlining of the word-count distribution. Avoid any kind of reptitions and try to find ways to connect things together so you don't have to restate them (like, you might point out how Asuka and Shinji seem like opposing sides of the same coin). Here's some examples I can use taken from what you've written so far: To begin with, the film begins with an infamous scene of the distraught protagonist, Shinji, masturbating to the body of his comatose friend Asuka, which sets the tone for what is to follow. --- "To begin with" and "the film begins" is an unneeded repetition. I would leave out "sets the tone for what's to follow" unless you plan on following up this comment on how what follows connects to it. Even then, I might leave it out. One thing I hate about just mentioning this scene is that it doesn't really describe HOW Anno portrays this masturbation, which is arguably more important than just recognizing that it happens (for instance, Anno never actually SHOWS Shinji doing it, but merely implies it through sound--his moans--combined with shots of the room and a closing shot of semen on his hands). The rest of the narrative is marked by brief moments of triumph only to be ultimately subverted, as well as a prevailing sense of helplessness. -- Perhaps too conclusive. Like I said, it would probably be best to set whatever theme you're tackling as the conclusion and spend most of the paper arguing for it, rather than trying to cover a lot of ground and thinning it out. Asuka miraculously manages to break out... The rest of the characters can only watch. Eventually, the entire population of the world... The remaining characters can only watch in horror... --Another repetition. I would try to avoid "narrating" what's happening and keep descriptions to a certain minimum. A kind of "nothing but the facts" approach. the viewers are only left with an ambiguous final scene of Shinji and a mysteriously returned Asuka lying on a desolate beach together, seemingly the only two people in existence, . Upon realizing the presence of Asuka, Shinji begins to choke her, but stops. Asuka then says, “I feel sick,” and without any further explanation, the film ends. --I like the "matter of factness" here. I'd try to emulate this approach more and then save the more fanciful stuff for the analysis and interpretation. Hope this helped.
